explanation_md: `10 / 3` is `3` (integer division). `10 % 3` is `1`. The third line computes `10 - 3 * 3 = 10 - 9 = 1`, which is the same as the remainder — this is the mathematical relationship between `/` and `%`.

prompt_md: Which statement about `std::endl` vs `'\n'` is correct?

options.a: `std::endl` and `'\n'` are identical in every way.
options.b: `std::endl` ends the line and flushes the output buffer; `'\n'` only ends the line.
options.d: `std::endl` is a character literal; `'\n'` is an object.

explanation_md: `std::endl` outputs a newline character and then explicitly flushes the output buffer, which has a performance cost. `'\n'` outputs only the newline. For most programs, `'\n'` is preferred because it avoids the unnecessary flush.
`c` is initialized to `2` (a copy of `a`). Then `c = b` overwrites `c` with `3`. `a` and `b` are untouched. Assignment copies a value; it does not link two variables together.

prompt_md: A program reads an integer `n` from stdin and computes `n / 2`. The programmer expects the result to always be exactly half of `n`. Which input will reveal that this expectation is wrong?

options.a: `n = 4` — result is `2`, which is exactly half.
options.d: `n = 100` — result is `50`, which is exactly half.

explanation_md: When `n` is odd, integer division drops the fractional part: `7 / 2 = 3`, not `3.5`. The expectation breaks on any odd input. Even inputs like 4 and 100 happen to divide evenly, so they do not reveal the issue.

prompt_md: Which of the following correctly describes what `return 0;` at the end of `main` does?

options.a: It returns the value `0` to the operating system, signalling that the program completed successfully.
options.b: It resets all variables to zero before the program exits.
options.c: It causes the program to loop back to the start of `main`.
options.d: It is optional and has no effect in a modern C++ program.

explanation_md: `return 0;` exits `main` and reports the exit code `0` to the operating system, conventionally meaning success. Non-zero values signal errors. Modern C++ does allow omitting `return 0;` from `main` (the compiler inserts it implicitly), but including it explicitly is good practice for clarity.
